content:
How to use Agents-A1 for free?

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Austin Distel on UnsplashEveryone in AI has been obsessed with making models bigger.
More parameters, larger GPUs, and increasingly expensive training runs have become the default path toward better intelligence. But a new open-source model called Agents-A1 is taking a very different approach.
Instead of scaling the model itself, Agents-A1 scales something much more important , its ability to complete long, complex tasks as an AI agent.
Built by InternScience, Agents-A1 is a 35-billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model that claims to deliver performance comparable to frontier AI systems containing hundreds of billions, or even trillions of parameters.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If these results hold up, it could change how future agentic AI models are trained. Let’s understand why.

What is Agents-A1?
Agents-A1 is an agentic large language model designed for tasks that require planning, reasoning, tool usage, and executing multiple actions before arriving at an answer. Unlike traditional chat models that mainly generate text, agent models must:

• Search the web

• Use APIs

• Execute code

• Call external tools

• Plan multiple reasoning steps

• Verify intermediate results

• Continue until the objective is completed
This makes them significantly more capable for real-world automation. Agents-A1 focuses specifically on improving these long-running workflows instead of simply improving next-token prediction.

Scaling the Horizon, Not the Parameters
The paper introduces an interesting idea. Instead of asking:
“How do we build a bigger model?”
it asks:
“How do we make the model solve much longer problems?”
The authors call this Horizon Scaling. Traditional LLM training usually teaches models using relatively short reasoning traces. Agents-A1 instead trains on long-horizon trajectories, where every task contains:

• Actions

• Tool calls

• Observations

• Intermediate verification

• Final reasoning
Rather than learning only the answer, the model learns the entire decision-making process. This produces an AI that behaves more like an autonomous agent than a chatbot.

A Knowledge-Action Infrastructure
One of the biggest innovations behind Agents-A1 is its domain-grounded knowledge-action infrastructure.
During training, every problem is converted into a structured sequence containing:

• planned actions

• observations

• tool outputs

• verification results

• reasoning steps
Instead of seeing isolated examples, the model learns complete workflows. Think of it as teaching someone not only the solution to a math problem but every calculation, correction, and verification performed along the way.
This makes the reasoning process itself trainable.

A Three-Stage Training Pipeline
Rather than relying on a single training stage, Agents-A1 follows three different phases.
Stage 1: General Agent Alignment
The base model is first fine-tuned across multiple domains so it understands general agent behavior, instruction following, reasoning, and tool usage. This provides a strong foundation.
Stage 2: Domain Expert Teachers
Instead of one universal teacher model, InternScience trains specialized teacher models. Each teacher focuses on a different area such as:

• Scientific research

• Engineering

• Long-horizon search

• Tool usage

• Instruction following
Each teacher becomes an expert in its own field.
Stage 3: Multi-Teacher Distillation
Finally, knowledge from all expert teachers is distilled back into a single 35B model.
The paper introduces heterogeneity-aware optimization, allowing the model to learn efficiently from multiple domain experts without conflicting knowledge.
This creates one unified agent capable of handling diverse real-world tasks.

Native Tool Calling
Modern AI agents cannot rely only on internal knowledge. Agents-A1 is built to interact with external systems through native function calling.
It supports:

• Search engines

• APIs

• Code execution

• External tools

• Multi-step workflows
This allows it to solve tasks that require real-time information and iterative reasoning instead of generating answers purely from memory.

Massive 262K Context Window
Agents-A1 supports a 262,144-token context window.
This enables it to process:

• lengthy research papers

• large codebases

• complex documentation

• multi-session conversations

• extended reasoning chains
Long context is especially valuable for autonomous agents, where maintaining state across many reasoning steps is critical.

Strong Scientific Reasoning
Scientific reasoning is one of the hardest evaluation areas for LLMs.Agents-A1 performs particularly well across several scientific benchmarks. Some standout scores include:

• FrontierScience-Olympiad: 79.0 (Overall SOTA)

• FrontierScience-Research: 40.0 (Overall SOTA)

• HiPhO: 46.4 (Overall SOTA)

• HLE with Tools: 47.6 (Best among comparable 35B models)
These benchmarks evaluate whether a model can combine reasoning with tool usage instead of relying solely on memorized facts.

Excellent Long-Horizon Search Performance
Long-running search tasks are another major strength.
Agents-A1 scores:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

These tasks require multiple search iterations, planning, evidence gathering, and verification, exactly the kind of workflow autonomous AI agents need to perform.

Strong Instruction Following
Instruction following remains one of the most practical benchmarks for production AI. Agents-A1 achieves:

• IFEval: 94.8 (SOTA)

• IFBench: 80.6 (SOTA)
This indicates that the model handles detailed, multi-constraint prompts more reliably than many competing systems.

Engineering Performance
For software engineering tasks, Agents-A1 also performs competitively.
On SciCode, it scores 44.3, outperforming several similarly sized models.
Although larger frontier models still maintain an advantage on some coding benchmarks, Agents-A1 narrows the gap considerably despite operating with only 35B parameters.

Why the 35B Size Matters
Perhaps the most impressive aspect of Agents-A1 is not any individual benchmark. It is how small the model actually is. Despite containing only 35 billion parameters, it competes against systems such as:

• GPT-5.5

• DeepSeek V4 Pro

• Kimi-K2.6

• Step-3.5 Flash
Many of these models are substantially larger and require far greater computational resources. Agents-A1 demonstrates that better training strategies and stronger agent workflows can sometimes matter more than simply increasing model size.

Open Source and Developer Friendly
InternScience has open-sourced:

• Model weights

• Technical report

• Evaluation framework

• Benchmark scripts
The model supports popular inference frameworks including:

• Hugging Face Transformers

• vLLM

• SGLang
It also includes native support for reasoning parsers and tool-calling, making deployment straightforward for developers building AI agents.

Final Thoughts
Agents-A1 represents an important shift in how researchers think about scaling AI.
Instead of chasing ever-larger parameter counts, it focuses on teaching models to reason across longer horizons, coordinate multiple tools, and solve complex workflows from start to finish.
The result is a 35B open-source model that delivers impressive performance across scientific reasoning, long-horizon search, instruction following, and agentic tasks, often outperforming much larger competitors.
As AI increasingly moves from chatbots to autonomous agents, models like Agents-A1 suggest that the future may belong not to the biggest models, but to the ones that can plan better, reason longer, and act more intelligently.
